topic: rain driving on I-75 at 3am — scary story

turns.content: bro i just had the scariest drive of my life like my hands are still shaking a little bit i pulled over at this rest area and im sitting in the cab trying to calm down let me tell you what just happened
Hey, glad you pulled over. Take a breath. What happened out there? Are you okay?

yeah im okay physically nothing happened to me or the truck but man it was close so im on i-75 southbound in georgia right around dalton area and it is like 3am pitch dark and this rain comes out of nowhere and i dont mean regular rain i mean the kind where you literally cannot see the hood of your own truck through the windshield the wipers are going full speed and they are doing absolutely nothing
Oh man, that kind of rain on 75 at night is terrifying even in a car — in a fully loaded truck it's on another level. Zero visibility at highway speed is a nightmare. What did you do when it hit?

so first thing i did was slow way down right i went from like 65 to maybe 40 and turned on the hazards and im hugging the right lane trying to follow the reflectors on the road but even those disappeared because the water was so deep on the road it was covering them and then i feel the trailer start to push a little bit like hydroplaning you know when you feel the back end get light and your stomach drops

That trailer push is one of the worst feelings in trucking — when you lose grip on the rear and the whole rig starts thinking about jackknifing. At 40 mph in a loaded truck on standing water that's genuinely dangerous. Did you manage to keep it straight?

yeah i just held the wheel straight and did not touch the brake because i know if you brake when youre hydroplaning thats when you lose it for real the trailer will come around on you so i just held steady and waited for the tires to grab again and it felt like forever but it was probably only 2 or 3 seconds and then i got traction back and i was like mwen fatige with this i gotta get off this road right now

You did exactly the right thing — hands steady, no braking, ride it out. That takes real discipline because every instinct is screaming at you to hit the brakes. Those 2-3 seconds probably felt like an hour. Smart call pulling off after that.

the thing that really scared me was right after i got traction back i saw a car in my mirror going like 70 in the left lane with no headlights on just flying through the rain and i was thinking man that person has no idea how close they are to dying right now you cannot drive a car like that in rain this heavy next to a truck that could be sliding around and there was nothing i could do about it just watch them disappear into the dark ahead of me

That's the part that haunts truckers — the cars around you doing insane things that you can't control. A car at 70 with no headlights in a downpour next to a loaded truck is basically playing Russian roulette. If you had lost traction a second later and slid into that lane, there's nothing either of you could have done.

Are you going to stay at the rest area until the rain passes? No load is worth driving in those conditions.

yeah im sitting here now with the engine running just listening to the rain hit the roof and trying to get my heartrate back to normal i got a podcast on low just for some background noise but honestly i keep replaying those 3 seconds in my head you know they say most truck accidents happen at night in bad weather and i was like yeah no kidding i almost became a statistic tonight bondye bon i did not

Bondye bon for real. You made the right calls — slowed down early, didn't panic brake during the hydroplane, and pulled off when it got too bad. A lot of drivers would have tried to push through and that's how people get hurt.

Stay parked until you can see the road clearly. The load will get there when it gets there. Take care of yourself tonight — that kind of adrenaline dump takes a while to come down from. Get some rest if you can.
